lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contract manufacturers, co-manufacturers, and private-label producers of potato chips, corn chips, popped popcorn, pretzels, and similar snack foods that repeatedly manufacture and package products for external brand owners, retailers, or foodservice customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturer-owned branded snack portfolios, captive plants, commodity-only sellers without a recurring outsourced-production relationship, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B production runs priced per unit, case, pound, or run under co-manufacturing, co-packing, and private-label agreements, often with additional product-development, sourcing, packaging, and logistics services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The industry combines proprietary-brand manufacturers with outsourced co-manufacturers. The lens is narrowed to contract, co-manufacturing, co-packing, and private-label operations because only those firms supply the recurring outsourced service specified by the default screen.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader food-manufacturing occupation mix, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in NAICS 311919. Food manufacturing is dominated by production roles such as food batchmakers and packaging-machine operators; I assign modest exposure to planning, scheduling, procurement, documentation, quality review, maintenance triage, and office work while treating line operation and material handling as largely physical. Broader-industry data may overstate exposure if small snack plants have fewer administrative specialists, or understate it where machine-vision quality review is unusually applicable.
a.rationale: The 2025 BLS snapshot reports 144,690 food batchmakers and 168,370 packaging and filling machine operators in food manufacturing, against 71,790 first-line production supervisors. These large physical-operating occupations constrain substitutable AI work, while workflow and information tasks around the line support a meaningful but minority opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS staffing table combined with worker-level time allocation for production planning, quality documentation, procurement, maintenance, customer service, and back-office tasks at 15-20 snack co-manufacturers.
caveats: BLS occupation figures cover NAICS 311, not 311919 or the contract-manufacturing subset.
The estimate excludes conventional non-AI robotics and counts only current tasks not already automated.

rho.rationale: Standardized scheduling, document drafting, demand forecasting, and exception triage can be deployed within five years, but food-safety validation, integration with older plant systems, thin management teams, customer-specific specifications, and required human accountability slow conversion of exposed work into realized labor avoidance. Census describes manufacturing robotics data as experimental and tracks robot presence only at broad industry levels, reinforcing the lack of a direct implementation measure.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before-and-after studies from snack manufacturers deploying AI scheduling, vision inspection, preventive-maintenance, and quality-documentation systems, with labor hours and false-reject or safety-event outcomes over at least 12 months.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in snack co-manufacturers.
Implementation may be lower in small plants with legacy equipment and higher in scaled co-manufacturers with modern MES and ERP systems.

e.rationale: Census counted 441 NAICS 311919 establishments in 2021, but does not classify them by branded versus outsourced model. Multiple industry operators explicitly offer private-label, co-packing, or co-manufacturing, confirming a real eligible segment; the base assumes that segment remains a minority because the code also contains regional and national proprietary-brand producers.
e.change_evidence: A complete census or commercial directory of the 70 injected LMM-band firms, coded from websites, customer concentration, and revenue mix for contract/private-label work, with multi-establishment firms deduplicated.
caveats: Public websites demonstrate existence, not population share.
Establishment counts are not firm counts and predate the injected firm estimate.
Some manufacturers mix proprietary brands and contract production; eligibility should be revenue-weighted in diligence.

s5.bridge: Census reports that over half of US business owners were 55 or older, while Project Equity reports that six in ten Washington business owners planned to sell within a decade and only 20% of companies listed with a leading broker sell. I translate those cross-industry owner-age, intention, and completion indicators into a five-year probability of a qualifying control transfer for eligible snack co-manufacturers, excluding closures, failed listings, family succession without control change, and internal reorganizations. The broad intention statistic likely overstates completed transfers; the broker statistic may understate larger, profitable LMM firms.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership creates supply, but intent does not equal a transferable control sale. The midpoint therefore sits well below a mechanical half of the ten-year sell-intention statistic.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and proprietary food-industry banker data linking five years of closed control transactions and failed processes to the starting population of eligible US snack co-manufacturers.
caveats: Owner-age data are all-industry and not limited to LMM firms.
The sale-intention evidence is Washington-specific and the broker completion figure is not snack-industry-specific.
The estimate excludes plant closures and asset sales that do not transfer the operating firm.

q.rationale: Co-manufacturing is commonly sold as an integrated, specification-driven service from sourcing through packaged goods. An operator can retain part of implemented productivity during a contract term, but sophisticated retailers and brand owners can demand open-book costing, rebid contracts, or capture savings at renewal. Differentiated formulations, food-safety performance, switching qualification, and reliable capacity support retention above a pure commodity toller.
q.change_evidence: Twenty customer contracts and renewal histories showing term, indexation, cost-plus or fixed-price structure, audit rights, productivity-sharing clauses, rebid frequency, customer concentration, and realized gross-margin change after automation.
caveats: No public source quantifies contractual pass-through in this niche.
Retention varies sharply between proprietary formulations, turnkey private label, and commodity co-packing.

d5.rationale: Snack purchases are deeply habitual, and USDA historical research found 99% of households purchased some salty snacks, supporting a resilient category. Offsetting that, USDA's 2025 potato-availability note says total potato availability is about 20 pounds below early-2000s levels and describes slowed growth in frozen potato products. For the current mixed snack basket, constant-price, constant-quality quantity is therefore modeled as roughly flat with modest upside from convenience and product innovation and downside from health preferences or retailer rationalization.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Circana or NielsenIQ unit-volume data by salty-snack subcategory and channel, decomposed into price, mix, and physical units, plus customer forecasts from the eligible co-manufacturer subset.
caveats: The near-universal household-purchase evidence is from 1999 and establishes breadth, not current growth.
Potato availability is only one input category and is not identical to finished snack demand.
The estimate holds quality and price constant and therefore differs from nominal market forecasts.

o.bridge: FDA rules apply to covered food facilities rather than directly measuring the share of snack quantity requiring a contract-manufacturing operator. Because facilities must implement a written food-safety plan and preventive controls and qualified personnel must oversee key activities, I infer that nearly all outsourced year-five physical snack production still requires an accountable manufacturer even if software automates planning or inspection. This can overstate operator retention if brands insource production or fully automated plants consolidate volume, but software alone cannot manufacture and package the food.
o.rationale: The service basket culminates in physical, regulated food production. AI can change staffing and control systems but does not eliminate the accountable facility that procures inputs, runs equipment, verifies safety, packages product, and releases lots.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing surveys on insourcing plans, five-year co-manufacturing capacity announcements, and plant studies documenting lights-out production with allocation of legal food-safety accountability.
caveats: FDA requirements establish accountability but do not guarantee that the same independent operator retains the work.
Volume can shift to customers' captive plants or larger co-manufacturers even when an operator remains necessary.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is the outsourced private-label and co-manufacturing subset, not branded snack manufacturing as a whole. It combines repetitive B2B production with durable physical demand, but the AI-addressable wage base is limited because most plant work remains physical and regulated.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term AI is most credible in production scheduling, forecasting, procurement, specification and quality-document drafting, maintenance triage, customer service, and machine-vision-assisted inspection. Human operators, mechanics, sanitation staff, and qualified food-safety personnel remain central; legacy systems and validation requirements limit implementation speed.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price or per-unit contract terms can let the manufacturer retain savings until renewal, especially where formulation, qualification, reliability, and scarce capacity create switching costs. Open-book costing, retailer bargaining power, tenders, and contract rebids can transfer a material share of gains to customers.
narrative.firm_availability: Public evidence confirms an active ecosystem of co-manufacturers and private-label snack producers, but no source measures their share of LMM firms. Aging-owner evidence supports recurring sale supply, while weak broker completion rates argue against equating retirement intent with executable transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Snacking is broad and repeat-oriented, while contract customers continue to need physical production, packaging, food-safety controls, and accountable lot release. The quantity outlook is closer to stable than high-growth because health preferences, category maturity, and retailer assortment changes can offset convenience-led demand.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the true eligible-firm share, customer concentration and contract pass-through, and plant-specific implementation economics. Commodity inputs, recalls, allergen control, private-label bargaining, obsolete equipment, and customer insourcing can erase gains; AI should not be credited for conventional robotics already embedded in lines.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring outsourced production plus regulated physical accountability makes the service relationship hard to replace with software alone.
narrative.principal_weakness: The addressable co-manufacturer subset is unmeasured and AI exposure is modest because production labor is predominantly physical.

sources.title: Snack Food and Beverages - Manufacturing Stats for the Big Game
sources.supports: Census reports 2021 NAICS 311919 sales/value of shipments of $26.533 billion, annual payroll of $1.942 billion, 37,250 employees, and 441 establishments.

sources.title: Food Manufacturing: NAICS 311
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 food-manufacturing employment including 144,690 food batchmakers, 168,370 packaging and filling machine operators, and 71,790 first-line production supervisors.

sources.title: Annual Survey of Manufactures Industrial Robotic Equipment: 2018-2021
sources.supports: Census describes plant-level measures of robot presence, worker exposure, purchases, and capital expenditures, tabulated only to broad manufacturing levels, and labels the product experimental.

sources.title: SnackCo of America - Full-service private label and co-packing
sources.supports: An industry operator describes private-label production, co-packing to customer specifications, co-manufacturing from raw-material sourcing through palletized goods, and product-development services.

sources.title: Ballreich Snack Food Company - Private Label and Co-Manufacturing
sources.supports: A snack manufacturer states that it develops, plans, produces, and delivers private-label and co-manufactured products for partners.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Visualizations - Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census states that over half of US business owners were age 55 and over according to the 2019 Annual Business Survey.

sources.title: New Research Details Effects of Silver Tsunami on Local Washington Economies
sources.supports: Project Equity reports six in ten Washington business owners planned to sell within ten years, about 15% pass to family, one-third of owners over 50 have trouble finding a buyer, and only 20% of companies listed by a leading broker sell.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: FDA states covered food facilities must maintain a written food-safety plan with hazard analysis and preventive controls, and management must ensure food workers are qualified for assigned duties.

sources.title: Taxing Snack Foods: What to Expect for Diet and Tax Revenues
sources.supports: USDA ERS reports that in 1999 about 91% of households purchased potato chips, about 96% purchased all chips, and 99% purchased some salty snack.

sources.title: From fresh to frozen: Potato per capita availability changes over time
sources.supports: USDA ERS reports that annual US potato availability is about 20 pounds below the early 2000s and notes slowed growth in frozen potato products.

sources.title: Registration of Food Facilities and Other Submissions
sources.supports: FDA states facilities manufacturing, processing, packing, or holding food for US consumption must register, allow inspection, and renew registration every other year.
